output:
## Answer: **(C)** — 10,015 seconds (~167 minutes), 33% speedup

### How pipelining works here

In a 3-stage pipeline, once the pipeline is **full**, a new record completes every clock cycle equal to the **slowest stage** (10ms for Stage 2).

**Sequential (no pipelining):**
```
Record 1: [S1: 2ms][S2: 10ms][S3: 3ms]
Record 2:                               [S1: 2ms][S2: 10ms][S3: 3ms]
Total per record: 15ms
```

**Pipelined:**
```
Record 1: [S1: 2ms][  S2: 10ms  ][S3: 3ms]
Record 2:          [S1: 2ms][  S2: 10ms  ][S3: 3ms]
Record 3:                   [S1: 2ms][  S2: 10ms  ][S3: 3ms]
                   ↑ new record exits every 10ms (bottleneck)
```

### The math

The standard pipeline throughput formula:

```
Total = (first record latency) + (N − 1) × (bottleneck stage)
      = 15ms + 999,999 × 10ms
      ≈ 10,000,015ms ≈ 10,000 seconds
```

The **+15** in option C represents the **pipeline fill/drain overhead** — the first record must pass through all three stages (15ms) before steady-state throughput kicks in. While numerically negligible here (15ms out of 10,000s), acknowledging it is the technically complete answer.

**Speedup:**
```
(15,000 − 10,000) / 15,000 = 33.3%
```

### Why not the others

| Option | Problem |
|--------|---------|
| **(A)** | Close, but ignores pipeline fill time — incomplete model |
| **(B)** | Assumes all stages fully overlap to 5ms — impossible since Stage 2 alone takes 10ms |
| **(D)** | Pipelining explicitly enables overlap between independent records; no data dependency prevents it |

The key insight: **pipelining doesn't reduce latency per record** (still 15ms each), it increases **throughput** by overlapping stages across different records, bounded by the slowest stage.
